¥ 24. A 55-year-old woman has had vague pain in the left lower quadrant of the abdomen for 4 months and foul-smelling urine for 2 weeks. She appears thin. There is mild
tenderness in the left lower quadrant of the abdomen. Rectal examination shows tenderness with no masses. Hematocrit is 29%. Urinalysis shows 50-100 WBC/hpf and 4+
bacteria. Urine culture grows greater than 100,000 colonies/mL of mixed fecal flora. Test of the stool for occult blood is positive. Which of the following is the most likely
underlying diagnosis?

options.A: mebiasis (Choice A) is usually caused by Entamoeba histolytica and presents with subacute onset of bloody diarrhea and abdominal pain. Stool polymerase chain reaction,
options.C: olon cancer is the most likely cause of this patient's foul-smelling urine growing fecal bacteria and positive test for occult blood in the stool. Colon cancer is the third most common
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, C, D, and E.
options.F: amilial polyposis (Choice C) is a genetic syndrome that causes colon cancer in almost 100% of patients if a colectomy is not performed prophylactically. Patients usually present in

explanation:
Colon cancer is the most likely cause of this patient's foul-smelling urine growing fecal bacteria and positive test for occult blood in the stool.

Colon cancer is the third most common
cancer in adults and most often presents after the age of 50 years, although patients with hereditary disorders or a positive family history of colon cancer frequently develop cancer
at a younger age.

Colorectal cancer is one of the few malignancies for which routine screening using colonoscopy can meaningfully change outcomes, as lesions that are detected
early are often curable, while patients who present with symptoms referable either to the primary tumor or to metastatic disease have a far worse prognosis.

In the case of this
patient, the presence of fecal flora in the urine suggests a colovesical or other gastrointestinal-urinary fistula, which likely developed following erosion of a primary colonic mass into
the bladder.

The concomitant presence of a positive fecal occult blood stool test lends further evidence to the presence of a colonic mass, while her thin appearance, the duration of
her symptoms, and a hematocrit of 29% suggest that the tumor has been present for at least 4 months and likely much longer.

The next step in her evaluation should include a CT
scan of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast and a colonoscopy.

Treatment is largely determined by the extent of disease.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 25. A 68-year-old man has had an enlarging lump under his tongue over the past 3 months. The lesion is now ulcerated and somewhat painful. He quit smoking cigarettes
10 years ago. There is a mass adjacent to the inferior dental arch extending to the inferior surface of the tongue. Submental lymph nodes are palpable, but there is no neck
adenopathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.E: Wide excision of the mass with radical neck dissection
options.B: iopsy of the lymph nodes (Choice D) is useful for staging of oropharyngeal carcinoma but is not as useful for establishing the initial diagnosis. Biopsy of the primary, sublingual
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, D, and E.

explanation:
Oropharyngeal carcinoma is common and may occur in the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, or nasal cavity.

Squamous cell carcinoma is the most common type of oropharyngeal
carcinoma and is strongly associated with tobacco use and infection with human papillomavirus (HPV).

Patients may present with an oropharyngeal lump or nodule, non-healing
ulcer, dysphagia, odynophagia, and weight loss.

Establishing the diagnosis with a biopsy is essential to guiding further management.

Staging can be aided by cervical sentinel

lymph node biopsy and imaging with CT or MRI scanning.

Early-stage disease may be managed successfully with surgical excision, although more advanced lesions usually
require a combined approach with surgery, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 26. A 67-year-old man remains intubated and mechanically ventilated 6 hours after admission to the hospital for hematemesis. He has alcoholic cirrhosis and was brought to the
emergency department 6 hours after he vomited a copious amount of blood. He has received 4 units of packed red blood cells. His current temperature is 37.4°C (99.3°F)
pulse is 105/min, and blood pressure is 130/75 mm Hg. Examination shows peripheral muscle wasting. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. The abdomen
is moderately distended and nontender. There is no fluid wave. His hematocrit is 29%. Placement of a nasogastric tube yields dark blood. Which of the following is the most
appropriate next step in management?

options.C: irrhosis can be complicated by portal hypertension, which often leads to the development of esophageal or gastric varices that may rupture and lead to massive, life-threatening
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, D, and E.

explanation:
Cirrhosis can be complicated by portal hypertension, which often leads to the development of esophageal or gastric varices that may rupture and lead to massive, life-threatening
hematemesis.

Additionally, alcoholic gastritis independent of cirrhosis and portal hypertension is a common source of gastrointestinal bleeding.

In cirrhosis, obliteration of the
hepatic sinusoids through progressive fibrosis increases the resistance to blood flow through the liver, which is transmitted to the portal venous system causing portal hypertension.

When engorged secondary to portal hypertension, esophageal veins can be visualized on esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) as vertical dilations underneath the esophageal
mucosa, representing dilated submucosal veins (termed ‘varices’).

Varices are usually treated endoscopically by band ligation, and patients with cirrhosis must be screened for
them, as the risk of life-threatening upper gastrointestinal bleeding is high.

The definitive emergent treatment for any source of upper gastrointestinal bleeding is EGD, as it permits
both identification of (and sometimes biopsy of) the source (diagnostic) and intervention to control bleeding (therapeutic).

This patient presents with hematemesis likely from a
ruptured varix, gastritis, or similar upper gastrointestinal source.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®

Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 28. A 16-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents after they noticed a small amount of bright red blood in his diaper 2 hours ago. The boy
awakened twice overnight screaming as if in pain, and the parents first saw the blood after the second awakening. The patient ate well and was playing normally prior to
going to bed. Ten days ago, he had an upper respiratory tract infection which resolved without treatment. On arrival, the child is sleeping and is difficult to arouse. Vital signs
are within normal limits. Palpation of the abdomen discloses mild tenderness and a mobile, 4 x 3-cm mass. There is no distention, rebound, or guarding. Bowel sounds are
normoactive. A small amount of blood and mucus is noted in the patient's diaper. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. An abdominal x-ray series
shows a nonspecific gas pattern. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.E: Upper gastrointestinal series with small bowel follow-through
options.I: ncorrect Answers: B, C, D, and E.

explanation:
Intussusception refers to the telescoping of a portion of the intestine into itself, most commonly at the ileocolic junction.

Symptoms include severe, intermittent abdominal pain,
emesis, and hematochezia.

Young children may be observed drawing their legs up toward their abdomen during pain episodes, and physical examination may disclose a palpable
abdominal mass, as in this patient.

Intussusception can be idiopathic or occur at a lead point, such as an area of inflammation or Meckel diverticulum.

The intussusception leads to
venous congestion, which results in intestinal edema with subsequent ischemia and necrosis.

Severe cases lead to intestinal perforation and peritonitis.

It most commonly occurs in
children less than 2 years of age and is often preceded by a viral infection.

Diagnosis is typically made with ultrasonography, which can disclose a “target sign” at the point of
intussusception.

Ultrasonography can also be used in conjunction with an air enema as both a diagnostic and therapeutic procedure pair for intussusception that has not
progressed to perforation.

29. A 76-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of a 3-day history of increasing abdominal
distention and left lower quadrant tenderness. The patient has a history of Parkinson disease and chronic constipation.
Medications are carbidopa-levodopa and docusate and enemas as needed. Vital signs are within normal limits. The
abdomen is distended and moderately tender to palpation over the left lower quadrant; no peritoneal tenderness, masses
hernias are noted. Results of laboratory studies show a leukocyte count of 18,000/mm%. An abdominal x-ray is shown. Which
of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Sigmoid volvulus is the most likely cause of this patient's abdominal distention and pain.

The x-ray of the abdomen discloses a grossly distended, air-filled colon with a dense,
vertically oriented line that represents two walls of the colon in apposition (coffee-bean sign).

Various other x-ray features, including the Frimann-Dahl sign, the presence of a
distended colonic loop over the liver (liver overlap sign), and the absence of rectal gas, are useful in supporting this diagnosis. A sigmoid volvulus results from twisting of the
sigmoid mesentery, which can lead to large bowel obstruction and perforation or intestinal necrosis if not appropriately treated.

Patients commonly present with symptoms
secondary to a large bowel obstruction, such as constipation, abdominal bloating, distention, nausea, and vomiting.

In patients without complications such as bowel ischemia or
perforation, treatment requires detorsion of the sigmoid colon and mesentery through sigmoidoscopy with placement of a rectal tube to facilitate stool passage and maintain
decompression.

Recurrent sigmoid volvulus may require surgical colopexy or sigmoid colectomy.

30. A hospitalized 6-week-old girl with respiratory distress syndrome of the newborn has sudden episodes of apnea and temperature
instability. Delivery was at 26 weeks' gestation. She has been exclusively fed with a cow milk-based formula. She appears pale and
lethargic. Her temperature is 35.8°C (96.4°F), pulse is 160/min, respirations are 64/min, and blood pressure is 65/35 mm Hg.
Capillary refill time is 6 seconds. Abdominal examination shows generalized distention and erythema around the umbilical stump.

An abdominal x-ray is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Necrotizing enterocolitis presents with decreased feeding tolerance, abdominal tenderness, distention, emesis, diarrhea, and hematochezia, most commonly in preterm infants,
especially those with very low birth weights.

If a feeding tube is present, gastric residual volumes may be high or there may be bilious drainage.

In neonates, signs of severe
systemic illness and shock include lethargy, temperature instability, apnea, and hypotension, some of which are seen in this patient.

Laboratory evaluation will typically demonstrate
leukocytosis, anemia. thrombocytopenia. and sians of disseminated intravascular coaaulopathy with increased clotting times. as seen in this patient.

It is caused by necrosis of the

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

An abdominal x-ray is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, C, and E.
options.H: emorrhagic disease of the newborn (Choice B) is caused by a deficiency of vitamin K, a cofactor in the coagulation cascade, that leads to life-threatening bleeding. Newborns

explanation:
Necrotizing enterocolitis presents with decreased feeding tolerance, abdominal tenderness, distention, emesis, diarrhea, and hematochezia, most commonly in preterm infants,
especially those with very low birth weights.

If a feeding tube is present, gastric residual volumes may be high or there may be bilious drainage.

In neonates, signs of severe
systemic illness and shock include lethargy, temperature instability, apnea, and hypotension, some of which are seen in this patient.

Laboratory evaluation will typically demonstrate
leukocytosis, anemia, thrombocytopenia, and signs of disseminated intravascular coagulopathy with increased clotting times, as seen in this patient.

It is caused by necrosis of the
intestinal mucosa, which allows the invasion of gas-forming bacteria into the bowel wall.

This leads to pneumatosis intestinalis on abdominal x-ray, which is diagnostic of necrotizing
enterocolitis in a newborn.

Necrotizing enterocolitis can progress to bowel necrosis with perforation and peritonitis if not treated.

Treatment includes intravenous antibiotic therapy,
bowel rest with fluid replacement, gastric decompression, and serial abdominal examinations.

Surgical resection is a consideration for advanced cases.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 31. A 45-year-old man comes to the office because of a 1-month history of rectal bleeding that occurs with bowel movements. The patient states the blood is mixed with the
stool. He does not report any pain with bowel movements and has not experienced any weight loss or fatigue. Medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications.
There is no family history of cancer. Vital signs are within normal limits. Results of abdominal and digital rectal examination show no abnormalities. Test of the stool for occult
blood is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.E: Repeat test of stool for occult blood in 1 month
options.B: arium enema (Choice A) may be useful in the diagnosis of diverticulosis, colorectal cancer, or inflammatory bowel disease. However, colonoscopy is the preferred initial study, as it
options.C: T colonography (Choice D) is a newer, minimally invasive diagnostic study for evaluating the luminal aspect of the colon. While it has a role in screening for polyps and colorectal
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, D, and E.

explanation:
Isolated rectal bleeding can be caused by numerous etiologies.

The more severe causes, such as malignancy and inflammatory bowel disease, benefit from early identification and
treatment.

Thus, the patient should next undergo colonoscopy.

Colonoscopy allows for direct visualization of lesions, collection of biopsy samples, and immediate therapeutic

interventions if needed.

Patients with active hemorrhage or hemodynamic instability require hospitalization and urgent endoscopic evaluation, while chronic, intermittent bleeding
can be evaluated in the outpatient setting.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 32. A 47-year-old homeless woman with alcoholism is brought to the emergency department after vomiting dark maroon fluid three times during the past 6 hours. Temperature is
37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 130/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 80/40 mm Hg. There is dark, crusted vomitus around her mouth, and scleral icterus.
Abdominal examination shows ascites. Mental status examination shows short-term memory loss; she is oriented to person and place but not to time. Test of the stool for
occult blood is positive. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin 7 g/dL
Leukocyte count 12,000/mm3
Serum
Total bilirubin 5 mg/dL
Direct 3.5 mg/dL

Administration of fluids is begun. Upper endoscopy shows esophageal varices with evidence of recent bleeding; no other abnormalities are noted. Which of the following is
the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: xam Section : Item 32 of 50 National Board of Medical Examiners®
options.H: emoglobin 7 g/dL
options.D: Placement of a transjugular portosystemic stent
options.B: anding involves placement of small elastic bands to ligate the bleeding vessel. Sclerotherapy involves injection of a sclerosing solution in the vicinity of the vessel. Risk factors for
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, C, and D.

explanation:
Variceal bleeding is a major cause of mortality in patients with cirrhosis, as patients can suffer large volume hemorrhage in a short period of time.

In addition to volume
resuscitation, transfusion of blood products, and airway protection, emergent upper esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) is required to achieve hemostasis.

Endoscopic
interventions are the definitive treatment for active variceal hemorrhage.

The two most common methods are variceal banding (also called variceal ligation) and sclerotherapy.

Banding involves placement of small elastic bands to ligate the bleeding vessel.

Sclerotherapy involves injection of a sclerosing solution in the vicinity of the vessel.

Risk factors for
variceal bleeding include the varix size, anatomical appearance, coagulopathy, and severity of cirrhosis.

Additionally, patients with variceal bleeding in the setting of cirrhosis have
improved outcomes with administration of somatostatin analogues (octreotide), antibiotics, and proton pump inhibitors.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 33. A previously healthy 37-year-old woman comes to the physician 6 hours after the onset of severe abdominal pain, fever, and chills. She takes no medications. Her
temperature is 38.6°C (101.5°F). Abdominal examination shows severe right upper quadrant and epigastric tenderness. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin 13 g/dL
Hematocrit 39%
Leukocyte count 18,000/mm with a left shift
Serum
Total bilirubin 1.2 mg/dL
Alkaline phosphatase 69 U/L
AST 19 U/L
ALT 15 U/L
Amylase 115 U/L

Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.H: ematocrit 39%
options.A: bdominal ultrasonography is the first-line diagnostic examination for the evaluation of potential acute cholecystitis. Acute cholecystitis occurs secondary to obstruction of the cystic

explanation:
Abdominal ultrasonography is the first-line diagnostic examination for the evaluation of potential acute cholecystitis.

Acute cholecystitis occurs secondary to obstruction of the cystic
duct from a gallstone, which results in subsequent inflammation of the gallbladder wall.

It typically presents with fever, right upper quadrant abdominal pain, and tenderness to
palpation, often in a patient with a history of gallstones.

It is occasionally preceded by symptoms of biliary colic from cholelithiasis.

Characteristic ultrasonography findings of acute
cholecystitis include gallbladder wall thickening and hyperemia, pericholecystic fluid, the presence of gallstones, dilated biliary ducts, and a sonographic Murphy sign (pain with
compression directly over the gallbladder).

If ultrasonography findings are equivocal, a HIDA scan, a nuclear medicine scan that evaluates for patency of the cystic duct and biliary
tree, can be performed.

Treatment includes supportive therapy, intravenous fluids, intravenous antibiotics, and urgent cholecystectomy.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 35. An 18-year-old man comes to the emergency department 48 hours after the sudden onset of right groin pain while lifting a heavy sofa. The pain has increased in intensity
since onset, and he has vomited twice during the past hour. His temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is
110/70 mm Hg. The right scrotum is edematous, slightly erythematous, acutely tender, and full. The abdomen is soft and mildly distended; bowel sounds are hyperactive.
X-rays of the abdomen show a few air-fluid levels in the small bowel. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: xam Section : Item 35 of 50 National Board of Medical Examiners®
options.A: pplication of an ice pack and elevation of the scrotum (Choice A) are not the appropriate next steps in management of this patient. This patient has signs of systemic illness, with
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, C, and D.

explanation:
An inguinal hernia occurs when a loop of bowel protrudes through the abdominal wall at and around the inguinal canal.

Symptoms include a bulge in the groin or scrotum, with
associated heaviness or a dull pain, often worsened with increased abdominal pressure during straining.

Risk factors for their development include male sex, advanced age, chronic
cough or constipation, smoking, and abdominal wall injury.

Congenital hernias are caused by failure of the processus vaginalis to close, whereas acquired hernias are caused by a
weakness in the abdominal musculature.

Indirect inguinal hernias are the most common type of hernia, often congenital, and occur when the hernia protrudes through the internal
ring of the inguinal canal.

Direct inguinal hernias are more commonly acquired and occur within Hesselbach’s triangle (bordered by the inguinal ligament, inferior epigastric vessels,
and the rectus abdominis muscle).

Incarceration occurs when the bowel loop is unable to reduce through the abdominal wall opening, which leads to edema and venous congestion
of the bowel.

Strangulation can occur with subsequent obstruction, ischemia, and necrosis, making both incarcerated and strangulated hernias a surgical emergency.

Incarcerated
and strangulated hernias present with tenderness to palpation, erythema, fever, and signs of bowel obstruction, such as emesis, abdominal distension, and air-fluid levels on x-ray.

This patient presents with an erythematous, tender, full scrotum associated with fever and emesis after experiencing the abrupt onset of groin pain while straining, making a
strangulated hernia the likely diagnosis.

The patient should undergo immediate surgical exploration to reduce the hernia or remove the ischemic bowel.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 36. A hospitalized 77-year-old woman has had fever for the past 3 days. She underwent surgical repair of a perforated esophagus 4 weeks ago and has had a prolonged ileus.
She received the diagnosis of pneumonia 16 days ago and has been intubated and mechanically ventilated since then. She is currently receiving broad-spectrum antibiotic
therapy and parenteral nutrition. Her current temperature is 39°C (102.2°F). A central venous catheter is in place. Examination shows no rash. Heart sounds are normal.
Blood cultures grow Candida albicans. |n addition to intravenous fluconazole therapy, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: xam Section : Item 36 of 50 National Board of Medical Examiners®
options.C: T scan of the abdomen (Choice B) may disclose intra-abdominal abscesses that can lead to persistent fevers in critically ill patients. However, intra-abdominal abscesses are
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, C, and D.

explanation:
Candida albicans is a fungal organism that most commonly causes oropharyngeal candidiasis, esophagitis, and vulvovaginitis.

Candidemia, or the spread of Candida into the blood,
is rare and often only seen in immunocompromised patients, with other risk factors including admission to the intensive care unit (ICU), central line access, and the administration of
total parenteral nutrition.

When present, candidemia often leads to severe sepsis with associated skin and ophthalmologic lesions.

Other presenting symptoms are similar to those
seen in bacteremia, such as fever, tachycardia, tachypnea, hypotension, and leukocytosis.

Central line catheters are often placed for the administration of vasopressors in critically
ill patients, as an alternative for venous access in patients with difficult to obtain peripheral access, and for the long-term administration of medications (eg, antibiotics or
chemotherapy).

Unfortunately, blood infections associated with central lines are a known complication often caused by skin flora such as Staphylococcus spp., although they can
also cause candidemia, as seen in this patient.

Treatment of central-line associated candidemia requires removal of the central venous catheter, along with the administration of
intravenous antifungal therapy.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 37. Forty-eight hours after undergoing mechanical aortic valve replacement, a 67-year-old man develops oliguria. His postoperative course was complicated by emergency
reexploration 18 hours after the valve replacement because of bleeding. At that time, he received 12 units of packed red blood cells and was hypotensive for 3 hours.
Anticoagulant therapy has been withheld because of concern about bleeding. Currently, his temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 102/min, and blood pressure is
110/68 mm Hg. Pulmonary capillary wedge pressure is 12 mm Hg (N=5—16). His serum urea nitrogen concentration is 56 mg/dL, and serum creatinine concentration is
3.1 mg/dL. On admission, his serum urea nitrogen concentration was 12 mg/dL, and serum creatinine concentration was 0.9 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most likely
cause of the oliguria?

options.E: Renal artery embolus from the valve
options.A: n acute febrile transfusion reaction (Choice A) (febrile, non-hemolytic transfusion reaction (FNHTR)) occurs due to the presence of preformed antibodies to donor leukocyte
options.D: ecreased urinary output following an episode of hypotension is consistent with a diagnosis of acute tubular necrosis (ATN). The cells of the proximal convoluted tubule, which are
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, C, D, and E.
options.H: ypoxia (Choice C) may be a cause of ATN; however, this patient has a known history of hypotension from hemorrhage, and no documented episodes of prolonged hypoxia.

explanation:
Decreased urinary output following an episode of hypotension is consistent with a diagnosis of acute tubular necrosis (ATN).

The cells of the proximal convoluted tubule, which are
responsible for a large portion of ion resorption from glomerular filtrate, are highly metabolically active and are especially sensitive to ischemic injury. ATN progresses through an
initial oliguric phase that may last for several weeks before recovery, followed by a subsequent polyuric phase.

Ischemic ATN may occur secondary to any type of shock state.

In

this patient, hemorrhage leading to hypovolemic shock and hypotension impaired renal perfusion.

Cardiogenic, obstructive, hypovolemic, and distributive shock etiologies can all
result in renal hypoperfusion. ATN may also occur secondary to nephrotoxic exposures, such as heavy metals, antibiotics, and toxic alcohols.

Urinalysis typically discloses muddy

brown casts.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 38. A 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by paramedics 30 minutes after his motorcycle collided with an automobile. He was ejected from the seat on
impact. He was wearing a helmet. He has severe pelvic pain. His pulse is 100/min, respirations are 28/min, and blood pressure is 120/70 mm Hg. The lower abdomen is
tender to palpation; there is no guarding or rebound. Examination of the pelvis shows severe tenderness on compression of the iliac crest. There is blood at the urethral
meatus, and ecchymosis over the scrotum and perineum. On digital rectal examination, the prostate cannot be palpated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.E: xam Section : Item 38 of 50 National Board of Medical Examiners®
options.A: motorcycle crash leading to pelvic pain, severe tenderness at the pelvis, and blood at the urethral meatus raises concern for a urethral injury. Urethral injuries are characterized
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, C, and D.

explanation:
A motorcycle crash leading to pelvic pain, severe tenderness at the pelvis, and blood at the urethral meatus raises concern for a urethral injury.

Urethral injuries are characterized
by difficulty voiding, blood at the urethral meatus, perineal ecchymosis, and potential pelvic fracture.

Straddle injuries can cause trauma to the anterior urethra, while pelvic fractures
more often injure the posterior urethra.

Depending on the location and severity of urethral injury, patients may require surgical repair or the placement of a urethral catheter to help
promote healing and prevent complications of urinary extravasation, infection, fistula, or stricture.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 39. A 28-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 3-month history of a mass in the upper outer quadrant of her right breast. The patient says the mass fluctuates in size
in relation to her menstrual cycle. She reports no pain or skin changes on or around the breast. Her family history is notable for a maternal aunt with a history of breast
cancer diagnosed at age 45 years. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination discloses a 1-cm mass that is mobile, well circumscribed, and nontender to
palpation. No axillary or supraclavicular adenopathy is noted. The remainder of the examination discloses no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate
next step in evaluation?

options.E: valuation of a new breast mass includes a thorough history and physical examination. Benign masses are commonly small, well-circumscribed, and mobile, whereas malignant
options.B: reast masses are common in women. While this patient likely has a benign tumor such as a fibroadenoma, a systematic approach to the evaluation of breast masses is pertinent.
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, C, and E.

explanation:
Breast masses are common in women.

While this patient likely has a benign tumor such as a fibroadenoma, a systematic approach to the evaluation of breast masses is pertinent.

Evaluation of a new breast mass includes a thorough history and physical examination.

Benign masses are commonly small, well-circumscribed, and mobile, whereas malignant
lesions are more likely to be large, poorly defined, adherent to underlying tissue, and associated with skin or nipple changes.

Further diagnostic evaluation of a breast mass
depends on the patient's age, as well as the clinical concern for malignancy.

In patients younger than age 30 years, such as this patient, ultrasonography is utilized as the initial
imaging modality to further analyze a palpable breast mass.

Cystic lesions are then observed or aspirated, benign-appearing solid lesions are observed, and malignant-appearing
lesions are biopsied.

For older patients, mammography is the initial imaging technique of choice.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 40. A 62-year-old woman has had a lump in her breast for 2 months. Her sister was diagnosed with breast cancer at the age of 65 years. Examination shows a 3 x 2 x 2-cm
smooth mass in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast. There is no lymphadenopathy. Aspiration of the mass yields dark green fluid. No residual mass is palpable.
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: reast cancer (Choice B) may be asymptomatic or present with a palpable, poorly defined, adherent breast mass associated with skin or nipple changes. However, most breast
options.C: ystosarcoma phyllodes is a solid mass and would not resolve with aspiration.
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, D, and E.

explanation:
Breast masses are common in women.

Evaluation of a new breast mass includes a thorough history and physical examination.

Benign masses are commonly small, well-
circumscribed, and mobile, whereas malignant lesions are more likely to be large, poorly defined, adherent to underlying tissue, and associated with skin or nipple changes.

Further
diagnostic evaluation of a breast mass depends on the patient's age, as well as the clinical concern for malignancy.

In patients younger than age 30 years, ultrasonography is
utilized as the initial imaging modality to further analyze a palpable breast mass.

For older patients, mammography is the initial imaging technique of choice.

Cystic lesions are then
observed or aspirated. A simple cyst that completely collapses after aspiration, as in this patient, requires no further management.

As well, cysts with aspirate colors of clear, brown,
and green are likely benign, while sanguinous aspirate should be sent to cytology because of the increased potential for malignancy.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 41. A 68-year-old woman comes to the office for a routine examination. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits.
Examination of the breasts shows no abnormalities. Mammography of the right breast shows a 15-mm area of microcalcification; core needle biopsy results show intraductal
breast carcinoma with two 1-mm areas of microinvasion. There are no abnormalities of the left breast. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in
management?

options.E: xcisional biopsy and radiation therapy (Choice B) could treat this patient’s breast carcinoma. However, radiation therapy may not be sufficient if her breast cancer has become
options.B: reast cancer is the most common non-dermatologic malignancy in women. It commonly presents with an abnormal, potentially palpable, breast lump, with or without associated
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, D, and E.

explanation:
Breast cancer is the most common non-dermatologic malignancy in women.

It commonly presents with an abnormal, potentially palpable, breast lump, with or without associated
skin or nipple retraction, although it may be asymptomatic.

Risk factors for the development of breast cancer include a family history of breast cancer, genetic mutation (eg, BRCA 1
or 2), early menarche, late menopause, nulliparity, obesity, exogenous estrogen use, and African American ethnicity.

If screening mammography discloses a lesion concerning for
malignancy, a percutaneous biopsy is warranted.

This patient's biopsy was consistent with intraductal breast carcinoma.

The most appropriate next step in management of this
patient is a lumpectomy to remove the mass and a sentinel node biopsy to evaluate for metastases given the evidence of microinvasion on biopsy.

This procedure would be both
diagnostic and therapeutic and influence treatment strategies such as adjuvant systemic hormonal therapy, biologic therapy, or chemotherapy.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 42. An afebrile 8-year-old boy has the sudden onset of pain in the right testis. Examination shows a swollen, tender, slightly erythematous right scrotum. Leukocyte count is
normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: xam Section : Item 42 of 50 National Board of Medical Examiners®
options.A: pplication of warm compresses and reevaluation in 12 hours (Choice B) is inappropriate. Delaying surgical intervention increases the risk of testicular infarction and necrosis,
options.B: Application of warm compresses and reevaluation in 12 hours
options.C: T scan of the right scrotum (Choice C) is not useful for the evaluation of testicular torsion. Duplex ultrasonography remains the gold standard modality for evaluation of testicular
options.I: ntravenous administration of antibiotics (Choice A) is not useful for the management of testicular torsion. Oral antibiotics are often used for the management of acute orchitis, which

explanation:
Testicular torsion is caused by rotation of a testis around the spermatic cord.

The twisting of the cord leads to compromise of the blood supply with resultant testicular edema,
ischemia, and subsequent necrosis.

It classically presents with acute testicular pain, tenderness to palpation, scrotal swelling, and a negative cremasteric reflex.

The patient may
develop nausea and emesis and/or lower abdominal pain prior to the onset of scrotal pain.

Physical examination may show a high-riding testis that is oriented transversely with
surrounding erythema.

Testicular torsion is typically a clinical diagnosis, although ultrasonography can be used for further evaluation if the diagnosis is unclear.

Ultrasonography will
demonstrate diminished or absent blood flow to the testes.

Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency and requires immediate urological evaluation when the diagnosis is suspected.

Surgery will allow for detorsion of the testis and orchiopexy (testicular fixation) of both testes to prevent recurrent torsion.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 43. A 57-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a nonhealing lesion on her foot for 8 weeks. She has type 1 diabetes mellitus treated with insulin. Examination
shows a nontender 2-cm plantar ulcer over the head of the first metatarsal. Dorsalis pedis pulses are normal bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely underlying
cause of the ulcer?

options.A: rterial insufficiency (Choice A) describes diminished flow of blood to the extremities secondary to atherosclerotic plaque. This most commonly affects the lower extremities.
options.C: hronic osteomyelitis (Choice B) may occur because of lower extremity wounds, and if osteomyelitis remains untreated, impaired wound healing can result. However, the proximate
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, C, and E.

explanation:
In patients with diabetes mellitus, chronic hyperglycemia results in the deposition of advanced glycation end products in capillary endothelium.

This leads to microvascular damage
to capillaries throughout the body, including the vasa nervorum of the distal extremities, which impairs nerve function.

While multiple mechanisms causing neuropathy are
hypothesized, the end result when considering somatic sensory fibers is impaired sensory perception, a type of diabetic neuropathy.

Diabetic neuropathy limits the healing response
of the distal extremities and reduces the patient’s awareness of injuries to the extremities, creating the conditions that result in the increased incidence of foot ulcerations in diabetic
patients.

Prolonged compression of soft tissues between bony prominences (such as the heads of the metatarsals) and hard surfaces (such as a shoe or floor) without offloading
can result in breakdown of dermal tissue and ulceration.

The feet and toenails should be carefully examined in all diabetic patients, especially those with known neuropathy, as
these patients are at an increased risk for developing diabetic foot ulcers and infections.

Poor foot and toenail hygiene may also lead to onychomycosis, overgrown nails, and
bacterial overgrowth that can increase the likelihood of skin breakdown and development of serious infections.

Patients should be counseled on regular foot care and examination.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 44. A 62-year-old woman comes to the office because she has had a nontender lump on the left side of her neck for 3 months. She has had no hoarseness or difficulty
swallowing. Examination shows a firm, palpable mass in the superior pole of the left thyroid lobe; on ultrasound, it is irregular and measures 2 1.2 cm. There is no neck
adenopathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.E: xcisional biopsy (Choice C) should be avoided when evaluating thyroid nodules. Thyroid tissue tends to bleed vigorously following excisional biopsy. Excisional biopsy also carries
options.C: T scan of the neck (Choice A) is unnecessary for localized thyroid nodules. Assessment of metastatic disease in the setting of confirmed thyroid cancer is best performed with an
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, C, E, and F.
options.D: irect laryngoscopy (Choice B) is not useful for the evaluation of a thyroid nodule, as the affected tissue cannot be visualized through this approach. While thyroid cancer may lead

explanation:
The initial evaluation for thyroid cancer includes a thorough history and a physical examination directed toward the identification of palpable thyroid nodules, regional
lymphadenopathy, or vocal hoarseness.

Following a physical examination, evaluation of the serum concentration of thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) may disclose a normal or
increased TSH concentration in the setting of a non-functioning nodule.

Hyperfunctioning nodules are rarely malignant and may be detected via a decreased TSH level or with
radioactive scintigraphy.

Large, irregular, non-functional nodules, or nodules containing solid and cystic components, may require a biopsy with fine-needle aspiration.

Excisional
biopsy is best avoided given the rich vascular supply of the thyroid gland and its tendency to bleed.

The Bethesda System for Reporting Thyroid Cytopathology can be used to
characterize the malignant potential of a nodule and to guide further management, which may range from observation to thyroidectomy.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 45. A 29-year-old man with a 6-year history of end-stage renal disease undergoes uneventful renal transplantation. There is immediate diuresis of the kidney with urine output
greater than 500 mL/h. Over the next several days, his serum creatinine concentration decreases to normal, and serum phosphorus concentration decreases to 1.5 mg/dL.
The decrease in the serum phosphorus concentration is most likely because of which of the following?

options.E: xam Section : Item 45 of 50 National Board of Medical Examiners®
options.C: hronic kidney disease results in secondary and tertiary hyperparathyroidism caused by derangement of calcium and phosphate homeostasis. As the failing kidney is unable to
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, C, and D.
options.H: yperglycemia (Choice B) can lead to hypophosphatemia in the setting of diabetic ketoacidosis due to osmotic diuresis. While renal transplant may provoke ketoacidosis in patients

explanation:
Chronic kidney disease results in secondary and tertiary hyperparathyroidism caused by derangement of calcium and phosphate homeostasis.

As the failing kidney is unable to
regulate the level of blood calcium and phosphorus, parathyroid hormone (PTH) concentrations increase to promote increased serum calcium from gut, renal, and bone absorption,
and to promote renal excretion of phosphorus. PTH levels remain persistently high in renal failure, and even after transplantation, the increased level can persist for several months
or even a year or more.

Following renal transplantation, the graft kidney will appropriately respond to PTH, which will result in reabsorption of calcium, and excretion of phosphorus.

There are multiple proposed mechanisms beyond PTH to explain post-transplant hypophosphatemia, though PTH is postulated to play a primary role.

Post-transplant, the kidney is
also able to produce active Vitamin D, a function also lost in end-stage kidney disease.

Vitamin D promotes gastrointestinal absorption of calcium and phosphorus, further
contributing to restored serum levels of both, and affecting feedback on the parathyroid gland to produce PTH.

The mechanism of electrolyte homeostasis following transplant is
complex, though PTH and Vitamin D, among several other factors, play key roles.

¥ 46. A 57-year-old man is being evaluated in the hospital 1 day after undergoing open reduction and internal fixation of a right hip fracture. He sustained a myocardial infarction
intraoperatively. Coronary artery angiography showed two occluded arteries; angioplasty and stenting were performed. He has hypertension, congestive heart failure
hypercholesterolemia, obesity, and major depressive disorder. Preoperative medications included metoprolol, atorvastatin, and fluoxetine; all have been continued since
admission. Currently, he is intubated and receiving morphine intravenously every 4 hours and 5% dextrose in 0.9% saline (100 mL/h). His temperature is 37.2°C (99°F)
pulse is 88/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 130/92 mm Hg. Examination shows a well-healing surgical incision on the right hip. There is sacral edema.
Preoperatively, his serum sodium concentration was 134 mEq/L. Laboratory studies today show:

question:

Surgery Self-Assessment

Sodium 40 mEq/L
Osmolality 500 mOsmol/kg H,O

Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's hyponatremia?

options.E: ctopic ADH (vasopressin) release (Choice B) is most frequently encountered in the setting of small cell lung carcinoma. While increased secretion of ADH from a non-pituitary
options.A: ppropriate secretion of ADH occurs in patients who are hypovolemic or those with conditions such as cirrhosis, renal failure, or heart failure that leads to decreased effective
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, C, and E.
options.D: ecreased total body sodium (Choice A) is incorrect. Hyponatremia is almost always caused by an excess of water rather than a deficiency in total body sodium apart from rare

explanation:
Increased hypothalamic ADH release leading to increased free water resorption in the kidneys is the most likely cause of this patient's hyponatremia.

Hyponatremia is often
asymptomatic if chronic and slowly developing, although when acute, it is associated with central nervous system symptoms such as headache, nausea, vomiting, confusion,
delirium, weakness, seizures, and coma.

The cause of hyponatremia can usually be determined by assessing the patient’s volume status in conjunction with their urine and serum
sodium concentration and osmolality.

In all instances of hypoosmolar hyponatremia, the appropriate physiologic response is to excrete dilute urine, which is reflected by a low urine
osmolality (<100 mOsmol/kg HO).

Urine osmolality that is substantially above this threshold suggests the presence of ADH, which can either be appropriate or inappropriate.

Appropriate secretion of ADH occurs in patients who are hypovolemic or those with conditions such as cirrhosis, renal failure, or heart failure that leads to decreased effective
circulating volume.

In either case, increased ADH secretion results in the resorption of free water in the kidneys to maintain blood pressure even at the expense of serum tonicity.

Rapid bedside assessment of the volume status can usually distinguish between hypovolemia and edematous states.

Patients who are euvolemic and have normal blood pressure
are more likely to have the syndrome of inappropriate ADH release (SIADH).

As the name implies, release of ADH is inappropriate as these patients have a normal volume status.

These patients also exhibit an increased urine sodium concentration, which is typically above 20 mEq/L.

In rare instances, euvolemic patients with high urine osmolality and urine
sodium concentration may have hypothyroidism or cortisol deficiency, but these are less common than SIADH and tend to present with other signs and symptoms.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 47. A 37-year-old woman with stage IV breast cancer is admitted to the hospital because of lethargy and right thigh pain for the past 3 days. Her pulse is 100/min, respirations
are 10/min, and blood pressure is 100/60 mm Hg. Physical examination shows dry mucous membranes. Mental status examination shows deficits in short-term memory. She
is oriented to person but not to place or time. Her serum calcium concentration is 14.9 mg/dL. Administration of which of the following is the most appropriate next step in
management?

options.A: potential cause of severe hypercalcemic crisis is malignancy-associated hypercalcemia, which can be secondary to the humoral secretion of parathyroid hormone-related peptide
options.C: alcitonin (Choice A) is a native hormone that inhibits osteoclast activity and reabsorption of calcium. It can be an adjunct treatment of hypercalcemia, but administration should not
options.F: urosemide (Choice B), a loop diuretic, acts at the renal tubule to promote fluid and electrolyte excretion, including calcium. It has a potential role in the management of
options.H: ypercalcemic crisis secondary to malignancy-associated hypercalcemia is the most likely cause of the patient's symptoms. Acute hypercalcemia can present with a broad
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, C, D, and F.

explanation:
Hypercalcemic crisis secondary to malignancy-associated hypercalcemia is the most likely cause of the patient's symptoms.

Acute hypercalcemia can present with a broad
spectrum of symptoms and can be life-threatening because of its effect on neuronal conduction, the cardiac action potential, kidney function, and volume status.

It commonly
presents with constipation, lethargy, weakness, and altered mental status.

It can result in a short QT interval and nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, leading to significant dehydration.
A potential cause of severe hypercalcemic crisis is malignancy-associated hypercalcemia, which can be secondary to the humoral secretion of parathyroid hormone-related peptide
(eg, squamous carcinoma of the lung) or from direct invasion and lytic lesions of the bone from osseous metastases (eg, breast cancer).

Treatment includes fluid resuscitation to
prevent renal failure and promote calciuresis, reduction of intestinal absorption of calcium, inhibition of bony destruction, and, if necessary, renal replacement therapy with dialysis.

Treatment therefore begins with the infusion of 0.9% saline to restore intravascular volume and dilute serum calcium, followed by the administration of osteoclast inhibitors such as
bisphosphonates and possibly calcitonin if further intervention is required to reduce calcium levels.

Loop diuretics may be appropriate after administration of intravenous fluids.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 48. Five days after undergoing emergency surgery for a perforated appendix, a 67-year-old woman has abdominal pain and nausea; she has vomited twice during the past 30
minutes. She has coronary artery disease and sustained a myocardial infarction 2 years ago; she has been taking oral nitroglycerin since that time. She appears confused
and agitated. Temperature is 38.8°C (101.8°F), pulse is 122/min, respirations are 23/min, and blood pressure is 88/50 mm Hg. There is no jugular venous distention. The
lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination shows no abnormalities. There is mild distention of the abdomen with tenderness over the suprapubic area. Rectal
examination shows mild tenderness on the right. This patient most likely has which of the following types of shock?

options.E: xtracardiac obstructive (Choice B) shock can occur with pulmonary pathology such as pulmonary embolism or tension pneumothorax. In tension pneumothorax, obstruction of
options.C: ardiogenic (Choice A) shock can occur in the setting of acute myocardial infarction, severe valvular dysfunction, severe heart failure, or arrhythmia. Pulmonary artery
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, C, and D.
options.H: ypovolemic (Choice C) shock occurs secondary to a significant reduction in intravascular volume, commonly related to hemorrhage or dehydration, which results in decreased

explanation:
This patient has signs of sepsis, a systemic inflammatory syndrome that may occur in response to infection, including tachycardia, tachypnea, and fever, with a probable intra-
abdominal source.

Sepsis can be complicated by shock and multiorgan failure with a high mortality rate.

Septic shock is characterized by vasoplegia (impaired response of the
vasculature to vasoconstricting stimuli) with markedly decreased systemic vascular resistance (SVR), tachycardia, increased cardiac output, oliguria, and lactic acidosis.

This
reduction in SVR is secondary to cytokine-mediated endothelial and vessel wall smooth muscle dysfunction.

The initial step in management of septic shock involves emergent
antibiotics and fluid resuscitation with isotonic crystalloids, such as 0.9% saline.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 49. Acomatose 44-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after sustaining a severe head injury in a motor vehicle collision. Her temperature is
36.2°C (97.1°F), pulse is 98/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. She is intubated and mechanically ventilated. The pupils are midrange and fixed, and gag and ciliary
reflexes are absent. She does not respond to painful stimuli, and there are no spontaneous respirations. She has a depressed frontal skull fracture, and there is blood in the
nares and right auditory canal. The family agrees to organ donation but requests proof of brain death other than the physical findings. Which of the following is the most
effective test to determine brain death?

options.E: ducational Objective: Brain death describes the irreversible cessation of whole brain functions. Guidelines issued by the American Academy of Neurology recommend assessing
options.B: CT scan of the head with contrast
options.D: eath is a challenging and common situation that physicians will encounter in practice. Though the exact nature of death remains an ongoing debate, the current generally
options.I: ncorrect Answers: B, C, D, and E.

explanation:
Death is a challenging and common situation that physicians will encounter in practice.

Though the exact nature of death remains an ongoing debate, the current generally
accepted definitions in the medical field are irreversible cessation of circulatory and pulmonary function or irreversible cessation of all functions of the whole brain, which is referred
to as brain death.

The American Academy of Neurology (AAN) issues guidelines to aid clinicians, medical systems, and patients’ families in accepting a declaration of brain death.

The three primary criteria for adult brain death are: known cause of coma and exclusion of any potentially reversible factors, a neurologic examination that tests for brain stem
reflexes and a central response to pain, and an apnea test which assesses whether the brain can drive respiratory functions in response to an increase in CO,.

If there is any
remaining uncertainty, certain ancillary tests may be performed to demonstrate an absence of cerebral blood flow.

The gold standard for cerebral blood flow studies is cerebral
angiography; transcranial ultrasonography, CT or MR angiography, and radionuclide brain imaging can also be used.

to as brain death.

The three primary criteria for adult brain death are: known cause of coma and exclusion of any potentially reversible factors, a neurologic examination that tests for brain stem
reflexes and a central response to pain, and an apnea test which assesses whether the brain can drive respiratory functions in response to an increase in CO.

If there is any
remaining uncertainty, certain ancillary tests may be performed to demonstrate an absence of cerebral blood flow.

The gold standard for cerebral blood flow studies is cerebral
angiography; transcranial ultrasonography, CT or MR angiography, and radionuclide brain imaging can also be used.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 50. An 18-year-old woman with ovarian germ cell cancer comes to the physician with her parents to discuss treatment options. Her condition has progressed despite

chemotherapy, and the chemotherapy has caused multiple adverse effects. Her parents have researched a new experimental treatment protocol that would involve bone
marrow transplantation but would only have a 5% 2-year survival likelihood. The daughter does not want to undergo transplantation because she says that she is ready to
die. Her parents are distraught and want to do everything possible to improve her prognosis. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: xam Section : Item 50 of 50 National Board of Medical Examiners®
options.A: sk the hospital ethics committee if the transplant should be performed (Choice A) is not necessary in this case, as there is no conflict between the different pillars of medical ethics
options.D: Proceed with the transplant at the parents' request
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, C, and D.

explanation:
The four pillars of contemporary medical ethics are nonmaleficence (do no harm), beneficence (do good), respect for autonomy (allow the patient to choose freely, as they are able),
and justice (fair access, distribution, and use of medical resources).

The ethical principle that applies most strongly in this case is autonomy, the right of the patient to ultimately
decide what treatments they do and do not receive.

In respecting autonomy, it is implied that the patient has capacity to make rational, informed, and voluntary decisions with
reason and understanding.

Society recognizes that age is a factor in the ability to make a fully reasoned choice with understanding, and the age at which patients are considered to
have full autonomy is determined by the legal system.

Generally, patients that are 18 years of age or older are considered to have full autonomy in medical decision-making.

In this
case, the moral decision is to respect the wishes of the patient.

Additionally, this course of action also respects the principles of nonmaleficence and beneficence, as the patient has
already experienced several adverse effects from treatment, and the additional experimental treatment is not yet known to provide any significant benefit with comparable mortality
rates.

options.E: ducational Objective: Contemporary medical ethics is guided by the principles of nonmaleficence, beneficence, autonomy, and justice. It is considered the moral duty of clinicians
options.A: sk the hospital ethics committee if the transplant should be performed (Choice A) is not necessary in this case, as there is no conflict between the different pillars of medical ethics

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 1. One day after undergoing uncomplicated splenectomy for management of injuries sustained in a motor vehicle collision, a 43-year-old man has the sudden onset of shortness
of breath while receiving a transfusion of packed red blood cells. He also has moderate pain at the intravenous site and moderate back pain. He has no other history of serious
illness. His medications are hydromorphone and subcutaneous heparin. He is diaphoretic. His temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 24/min, and
blood pressure is 120/60 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 97%. Examination of the skin shows no abnormalities. Breath sounds are clear
bilaterally; no wheezes or rhonchi are heard. Abdominal examination shows a well-healing surgical incision. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of this
patient's condition?

options.E: ndotoxin release from infused bacteria (Choice A) is more likely to present with rigors and fevers rather than flank pain and dyspnea. Additionally, transfusion-related sepsis is
options.B: Immune response to transfused plasma proteins
options.I: mmune response to transfused plasma proteins (Choice B) may refer to either an anaphylactic reaction to donor immunoglobulins, as might occur in patients with IgA deficiency, or

explanation:
Preformed antibodies to transfused red blood cells are the most likely cause of this patient’s acute onset shortness of breath and back pain, which is most concerning for an acute
hemolytic transfusion reaction (AHTR). AHTR is a life-threatening condition that occurs because of ABO incompatibility, which is often the result of clerical error.

Patients with type A
blood possess preformed antibodies against the B antigen on the surface of erythrocytes, while patients with type B blood possess antibodies against the A antigen.

Those with AB
blood lack preformed antibodies to both antigens, as they possess them on their own erythrocytes.

Patients with type O blood have antibodies against both A and B antigen.

Given
this, it is critical that patients receive the appropriate type of blood for their specific blood type.

As an example, patients with type A blood can only receive type A or type O blood
from a donor, but not type B or AB blood. ABO incompatibility leads to AHTR, which results in profound hemolysis and often presents with flank pain, fever, hemoglobinuria, and a
positive direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test, but may rapidly progress to disseminated intravascular coagulopathy (DIC), shock, and death.

Treatment includes immediate cessation of
the transfusion followed by supportive care.

The blood bank should be contacted immediately.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 2. A 62-year-old woman has shortness of breath immediately after beginning transfusion of fresh frozen plasma and 2 units of packed red blood cells to manage acute lower
gastrointestinal bleeding. She has been hospitalized for 24 hours. Five years ago, she underwent mechanical valve replacement for treatment of aortic stenosis. She has no
other history of serious illness. Her only outpatient medications are warfarin and lisinopril, which have been discontinued since admission. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F)
pulse is 110/min and regular, respirations are 28/min, and blood pressure is 100/60 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 82%. Crackles are
heard from the lung bases to the midlung fields. There is cyanosis of the upper and lower extremities. A urinary catheter is draining clear urine. A chest x-ray is pending.
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.E: xam Section : Item 2 of 50 National Board of Medical Examiners®
options.A: cute hemolytic transfusion reaction (Choice A) results from the transfusion of ABO incompatible erythrocytes. When this occurs, preformed antibodies against donor antigens on
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, C, and D.

explanation:
Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI) is the most likely diagnosis in this patient with sudden onset dyspnea, hypoxia, and fever following transfusion of packed red blood
cells and plasma. TRALI is a syndrome characterized by respiratory distress, often with associated hypoxemia, cyanosis, fever, and new infiltrates on chest x-ray, that develops
within 6 hours of transfusion of any blood product, including erythrocytes, fresh frozen plasma, or platelets.

Frequently, symptoms develop within minutes to hours after starting
transfusion.

The pathogenesis of TRALI is thought to involve abnormal priming of neutrophils in the lung, which, when exposed to inflammatory cytokines present in transfused
blood products, undergo widespread activation within the lung parenchyma.

Release of cytokines and reactive oxygen species among other inflammatory components from
activated neutrophils causes endothelial dysfunction and rapidly leads to non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema.

This contrasts with the pulmonary edema that develops in patients
who develop respiratory failure secondary to transfusion associated cardiac overload (TACO).

Treatment is with supportive care, which may include implementing non-invasive or
invasive ventilation, vasopressors, and antipyretics.

Importantly, patients who recover from TRALI can receive additional blood products, but these products should not be from the
same prior blood donor.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 4. A 20-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 50 minutes after being involved in a motor vehicle collision. He was an unrestrained passenger. On arrival, he is
alert. He says he has neck pain and numbness in both legs; he cannot move his legs. His temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 70/min, respirations are 20/min and
unlabored, and blood pressure is 80/50 mm Hg. Examination shows a patent airway. There is no jugular venous distention. There are multiple contusions over the trunk and a
bony deformity of the right calf. The cervical spine is tender. Sensation to touch is absent below the midchest. Abdominal ultrasonography for trauma (FAST) shows no free
fluid in the pelvis. X-rays of the cervical spine show subluxation of C7 on T1. X-rays of the chest and pelvis show no abnormalities. An x-ray of the right lower extremity shows
comminuted fractures of the tibia and fibula. CT scans of the abdomen and pelvis show a small contained hematoma. After infusion of 2 L of crystalloid solution, the patient's
blood pressure does not improve. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's persistent hypotension?

options.E: xam Section : Item 4 of 50 National Board of Medical Examiners®
options.A: ortic disruption (Choice A) can lead to rapid loss of intravascular blood volume and shock but is typically accompanied by tachycardia and extravasation of blood into the thorax or
options.B: leeding from the fractures (Choice B) can lead to hemorrhage within the compartments of the lower extremity, but hemodynamically significant hemorrhage is less common with
options.D: isruption of sympathetic fibers descending from the cardiovascular center in the medulla oblongata may also lead to bradycardia. Careful fluid resuscitation is essential for
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, C, and D.

explanation:
Neurogenic shock may occur following traumatic brain or spinal cord injury due to the disruption of autonomic fibers originating in the vasomotor center within the medulla
oblongata.

The vasomotor center receives input from baroreceptors in the aortic and carotid sinuses and regulates blood pressure via sympathetic fibers that descend through the
lateral aspect of the spinal cord before entering the lateral horn and sympathetic chain ganglia.

Loss of sympathetic innervation results in vasodilation and loss of vascular tone.

Disruption of sympathetic fibers descending from the cardiovascular center in the medulla oblongata may also lead to bradycardia.

Careful fluid resuscitation is essential for
maintaining perfusion.

Atropine may be trialed for the management of bradycardia following acute traumatic spinal cord injury, and vasoactive infusions such as norepinephrine may
be necessary to maintain perfusion.

Competing diagnoses such as blood loss in the setting of ttauma must be excluded or managed if present.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 5. An 18-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 3-week history of headache. The headache has worsened today and is now accompanied by mildly
blurred vision. Her mother has a history of migraines. The patient is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 91 kg (200 Ib); BMI is 34 kg/m2. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse
is 76/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 140/80 mm Hg. Funduscopic examination shows bilateral papilledema. The remainder of the examination shows no
abnormalities. A CT scan of the head without contrast shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: xam Section : Item 5 of 50 National Board of Medical Examiners®
options.I: ntravenous administration of methylprednisolone (Choice B) is useful for the acute management of optic neuritis, which generally presents with acute onset vision loss and ocular

explanation:
Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) is caused by inadequate resorption of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and is characterized by signs of increased intracranial pressure including
headache, papilledema, and increased opening pressure on lumbar puncture without gross abnormality on neuroimaging.

It is primarily seen in young women with increased BMI.

Prolonged increased intracranial pressure may cause chronic headaches and optic nerve damage leading to permanent vision loss.

Headaches that are secondary to increased
intracranial pressure often worsen when lying down and are relieved by standing.

The most common risk factors for the development of IIH include female sex and obesity.

Medications associated with IIH include tetracyclines, danazol, and growth hormone. CT scan is an important initial step in the evaluation of a patient with papilledema and
suspected IIH to exclude the presence of an intracranial mass.

If the CT scan is normal, lumbar puncture is the most appropriate next step to confirm the presence of increased
intracranial pressure.

Management of IIH includes weight reduction, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, therapeutic lumbar puncture, and in rare cases, surgical intervention with
cerebrospinal fluid shunting or optic nerve sheath fenestration.

during the past 5 years. He has hypercholesterolemia treated with atorvastatin. Examination shows no lymphadenopathy.
There is a 1-cm lesion on the nose. A photograph is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: asal cell carcinoma (BCC) is the most common form of skin cancer and is derived from the basal cells of the epidermis. The primary risk factor for developing basal cell carcinoma

ulceration commonly on the sun-exposed areas of the head and neck.

Subtypes of BCC include those that are less | invasive, such as superficial and nodular, and those that are

7. A65-year-old man comes to the office because of a 6-month history of an enlarging lesion on his left forearm. The lesion is not painful or itchy; it does not bleed and has not
changed shape. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. A photograph of the lesion is shown. Which of the following
is the most appropriate next step in this patient's management?

options.E: xcision with 2-cm margins (Choice B) or 5-cm margins (Choice C) is not necessary for the treatment of BCC. The risk of overall metastasis is low with BCC, and the risk of
options.B: asal cell carcinoma (BCC) is the most common form of skin cancer and is derived from the basal cells of the epidermis. The primary risk factor for developing BCC is ultraviolet
options.D: Sentinel node biopsy and excision with 1-cm margins
options.F: Sentinel node biopsy and excision with 5-cm margins
options.I: ncorrect Answers: B, C, D, E, and F.

explanation:
Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) is the most common form of skin cancer and is derived from the basal cells of the epidermis.

The primary risk factor for developing BCC is ultraviolet
light exposure, though age, fair skin, and family history also contribute. BCC typically presents as pink, pearly papules or nodules with rolled borders and central ulceration
commonly on the sun-exposed areas of the head and neck.

Subtypes of BCC include those that are less invasive, such as superficial and nodular, and those that are more
invasive, such as infiltrative and morpheaform.

In all subtypes, the risk of metastasis is low, though the tumors can invade locally and cause significant tissue damage.

Complete

surgical excision with a margin of between 5 mm to 1 cm is recommended as first line treatment for BCC located in noncritical areas that are amenable to complete resection with
adequate margins.

In critical areas of the face or other delicate regions, Mohs micrographic surgery may allow for complete excision with comparative sparing of normal tissues.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®
Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 8. A 47-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by paramedics after rescue workers found him on a snow-covered mountain. At the scene, he was alert and
oriented but shivering and unable to walk without assistance. He said he had become lost while cross-country skiing. On arrival, he is shivering and reports that his toes are
numb. His temperature is 36°C (96.8°F), pulse is 60/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 120/65 mm Hg. Examination shows pale toes bilaterally. Blisters are
noted on the 4th and 5th toes bilaterally. Sensation to touch is absent over the toes bilaterally. Pedal pulses are faintly palpable in both feet. Ankle brachial indices are 0.85
bilaterally (N>1). Tetanus toxoid is administered, and the feet are immersed in a 104°F water bath for 20 minutes. During immersion, his feet become painful. In addition to
administration of acetaminophen, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Amputation of all toes with blisters at 24 hours post injury
options.B: Application of nitroglycerin ointment to the feet
options.D: Immediate arteriography of the lower extremities
options.F: rostbite is frequently seen in patients who work in the cold, mountaineers and skiers, individuals stranded outdoors, and those experiencing homelessness. Frostbite causes tissue
options.I: ntravenous infusion of unfractionated heparin (Choice C) alone has not been shown to improve outcomes in patients with frostbite. Intravenous heparin can be used as an adjunct
options.A: pplication of nitroglycerin ointment (Choice B) is not as effective as immersion into warm water or warm compresses to help with vasodilation. Nitroglycerin has also been used in

explanation:
Frostbite is frequently seen in patients who work in the cold, mountaineers and skiers, individuals stranded outdoors, and those experiencing homelessness.

Frostbite causes tissue
destruction via cold-induced cell death, as well as reperfusion-related inflammation and tissue ischemia.

There are several ways to classify the severity of frostbite, depending on
degree of cyanosis, depth of tissue involvement, blistering, or findings on technetium-99m imaging.

Immediate management of frostbite involves removing cold or wet clothing,
transferring the patient to a warm environment, rapid rewarming by immersing the affected area in warm (but not hot) water, analgesia, and admission to the hospital for observation
and wound care.

Several adjunct therapies are often employed to limit tissue damage, including thrombolysis.

Initial signs and symptoms of frostbite are often unreliable in
determining the extent of injury; the clinical picture evolves as occult damage becomes apparent.

question:
National Board of Medical Examiners®

Surgery Self-Assessment

¥ 9. An 87-year-old man is transferred to the hospital from a skilled nursing care facility 6 hours after staff noticed black skin on his right leg. He has had three strokes during the
past 2 years. He has hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Current medications include metoprolol, glipizide, and clopidogrel. He does not smoke. He is not
communicative and responds only to painful stimuli. There are severe contractures of the lower extremities. Examination of the lower extremities shows black skin over the
right ankle and foot. Pulses are absent in the lower extremities distally. Extension of the hips is limited to 110 degrees; extension of the knees is limited to 70 degrees. Which
of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: xam Section : Item 9 of 50 National Board of Medical Examiners®
options.A: rteriography with contrast (Choice B) is useful for the assessment of vascular stenosis in patients with PAD or in patients with suspected acute limb ischemia. Surgical amputation
options.C: ilostazol therapy (Choice A) causes vasodilation and is useful for the treatment of claudication due to PAD. While it leads to improvement in claudication and in walking distance, it
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, C, and D.

explanation:
Peripheral arterial disease (PAD) refers to the abnormally diminished flow of blood to the extremities secondary to atherosclerotic plaque.

This most commonly affects the lower
extremities.

Risk factors include diabetes mellitus, increased age, tobacco smoking, hypertension, and dyslipidemia. A known history of atherosclerosis (eg, coronary artery
disease, cerebrovascular disease) identifies the likelihood of the condition being present in other locations as well.

Patients with PAD typically present with intermittent claudication,
which results from insufficient blood flow to the muscles during exercise.

As the flow of blood becomes progressively diminished with worsening atherosclerosis, tissue perfusion
may become inadequate to meet metabolic demands, and can present with pain at rest and the potential for ischemia and necrosis (gangrene) of the extremity.

Dry gangrene is
most common in patients with PAD, although wet gangrene may also occur.

Revascularization surgery may allow for salvage of the limb and improve rest pain or non-healing
wounds, but the presence of gangrene generally requires amputation.

In this case, an immobile patient with contractures will generally not suffer functional loss from amputation,
making it a suitable choice if it aligns with goals of care.

10. A 47-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-year history of a lump on her right ankle that seems to have increased in size during the past 2 months. The
lump is slightly painful only when she is active. She has had no other symptoms. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Examination
of the right ankle shows a 2.8-cm raised lesion that is firm and adherent to the bone. There are no skin changes or lymphadenopathy. Range of motion of the ankle is full.
X-rays of the right lower extremity are shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: xam Section : Item 10 of 50 National Board of Medical Examiners®
options.I: ncorrect Answers: A, B, C, and D.

explanation:
Malignancies of bone commonly occur in the lower extremities and present with chronic, often localized bone pain and a palpable bony or soft tissue lump or protuberance. A
minority of patients may present with fracture of the affected bone. X-rays are an important step in diagnosis.

This patient's x-ray demonstrates medullary and cortical bone
destruction of the anterior, distal, medial tibia, and malleolus with a periosteal reaction, indicating possible malignancy. A biopsy of the lesion is the most important next step in
management and can be performed with core needle or open biopsy technique.
